text: Preoperative markings are made on the patient's abdomen for the vertical rectus abdominis myocutaneous (VRAM) flap, in preparation for forming a double barreled wet colostomy during pelvic accentuation for rectal carcinoma.

The surgeon opens the abdomen through a midline incision and performs lateral to medial mobilization of the left hemicolon. The ureters are identified, and a high tie of the inferior mesenteric artery is completed. The sigmoid colon is transected, ensuring sufficient length for a double-barreled wet colostomy. Both ureters are fully mobilized, and the rectum is mobilized in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The bladder is mobilized, and the ureters are ligated distally. Using the ureters as a guide, a beyond TME resection of the pelvic organs is performed, and the internal iliac artery is ligated bilaterally.

The patient is positioned in the orthotomy position. An extra sphincteric perineal dissection is performed, continuing through the pelvic floor to connect with the abdominal dissection. The specimen is delivered through the perineal wound after completing the anterior dissection in front of the prostate, revealing the pelvic side wall structures.

The surgeon measures the urinary reservoir of the double-barreled wet colostomy to ensure it is approximately ten centimeters in length. The ureters are trimmed to the appropriate length, and a polysorb stay suture is placed to secure them.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis of the ureter to the colonic conduit using threomaxon sutures. A size eight infant feeding tube is carefully inserted into the ureter and secured with Velosorb Rapide sutures. The pediatric feeding tube is then fed into the colonic conduit, intended to be pulled out through the stoma. The right ureter is similarly anastomosed to the colonic conduit.

The colonic conduit is visible with both ureters anastomosed. A trefine is created, and the double-barreled colon conduit is brought out over a Jax catheter, facilitating the safe placement of the stoma bridge. The colonic conduit lies in the pelvis without tension on the colon or ureters. The colostomy is opened, and infant feeding tubes are extracted.

The surgeon flushes the tubes and places them into a sterile glove for convenience. The VRAM (Vertical Rectus Abdominis Myocutaneous) flap is developed and positioned in the perineal cavity to ensure a bulky and well-perfused closure of the perineum.

The surgeon performs simultaneous closure of the abdominal and perineal wounds. A large abdominal defect is addressed by placing a proline mesh. The stoma is visibly matured following the closure of the abdominal wound.
